Americans (and increasingly, British people) use you folks or you guys (to both Some British dialects have a separate plural form ye, youse or yiz. Many use thu or tha as a singular subject form and thee as a singular object form.

separate forms exist in certain varieties of English. Some speakers in Yorkshire Although standard modern English uses you for both singular and plural, 8  you:    different       singular      and   plural     forms Clever/Lucky (old) you! (This occasionally happens also with me.) You can be modified by adjectives in a few informal expressions such as Poor/ 7 Poor      you!

Give us a kiss, love.

as an indirect object).

In very informal British speech, us is quite often used instead of me (especially 6 us   meaning       'me' the listener.) We're going for a drink. Would you like to come with us? (We and us exclude Shall we go and have a drink? (We includes the listener.) Note that we and us can include or exclude the listener or reader. Compare: 5 inclusive       and   exclusive       we who waits.) Everything comes to him  who waits. (Considered more correct than ... to he correct than ... for you and I to meet soon.) 1 think it's a good idea for you and me to meet soon. (Considered more Section Pronouns             14 grammar •  175  singular they the traditional use of he/him/his in this situation (►  328).

and his or her are clumsy, especially when repeated, and many people dislike could be either male or female (as in the examples above). He or she, him  or her This singular use of they/them/their is convenient when the person referred to Every individual thinks they're different from everybody else.

Tell each person to help themselves to what they want.

Nobody was late, were they? Whoever comes, tell them I'm not in.

Somebody left their umbrella in the office. Would they please collect it?

If anybody calls, take their name and ask them to call again later.

If a person doesn't want to go on living they are often very difficult to help.

each, every, either, neither and no. They  has a plural verb in this case.

common after a person, anybody/one, somebody/one, nobody/one, whoever, They/them/their is often used to refer to a singular indefinite person. This is 1 Somebody           left    their      umbrella.

175  singular      they This dish takes two hours to prepare.

The bridge wasn't strong enough to drive over. (     .. .to drive over it.) not The  pie looked too nice to eat. (     . . .too nice to eat it.) not She's easy to please. (     She's easy to please her.) infinitive has just been mentioned (►    101.4).

Object pronouns are not normally used in infinitive clauses if the object of the He was carrying a box with cups in (it).

All the trees have got blossom on (them).

descriptive structures with have and with.

And in British English, personal pronouns can be dropped after prepositions in not 'Is that the manager?' ‘ I believe so.' (     ... I believe (it):) (For details, ►    585.) After certain verbs (e.g. believe, think, suppose), we  use so rather than it.

'It's getting late.' ‘ I know.' (     I know it) We   seldom put it after know (►  504.6 for details).

Seen Oliver? (= Have you seen Oliver?) Can't help you, I'm afraid. (= I can’ t...) sometimes left out at the beginning of a sentence. For details of this, ►    277.

However, in informal speech, subject pronouns and/or auxiliary verbs are reminded her of home.) not She loved the picture because it reminded her of home. (     ... because Personal pronouns cannot usually be left out (►   173.4).

11  leaving      out   personal       pronouns The  invitation was for Tracy and me. (More polite than .. .for me and Tracy.) not Why   don't you and I go away for the weekend? (     Why don't I and you-...?) It is considered polite to mention oneself last in double subjects or objects.

Dad said he didn't mind... (     Dad said Dad didn't mind...) However, pronouns need to be used to avoid repetition (►   287).
